title: Meet Hûˋloû₤se, the Medieval Woman Philosopher Who Turned a Doomed Love Affair into a Meditation on Ethics
description:
in History, Philosophy |   	June 18th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ill-fatôÙed romance of Abelard and Hûˋloû₤se may be a perôÙmaôÙnent culôÙturôÙal
body:
in History, Philosophy |   	June 18th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ill-fatôÙed romance of Abelard and Hûˋloû₤se may be a perôÙmaôÙnent culôÙturôÙal fixôÙture, but itãs worth askôÙing what any of us underôÙstand about Abelard or Hûˋloû₤se themôÙselves. Before the two ever crossed paths, Peter Abelard was already a celôÙeôÙbratôÙed philosoôÙpher in France whose classôÙes drew large and enthuôÙsiôÙasôÙtic crowds. This was, bear in mind, a time and place where arguôÙing realôÙism verôÙsus conôÙcepôÙtuôÙalôÙism amountôÙed to a specôÙtaôÙtor sport. A modôÙern framôÙing might analoôÙgize him to a cross between an intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal athôÙlete and a pubôÙlic intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal. That he would attract admirôÙing pupils is a givôÙen, but none seems to have exudôÙed the sheer allure of Hûˋloû₤se dãArôÙgenôÙteuil.
That allure, moreôÙover, was of the mind at least as much as of the body. ãA prodiôÙgy from a young age, Hûˋloû₤se was fluôÙent in sevôÙerôÙal lanôÙguages and renowned for her poetôÙry, musiôÙcal prowess, and fiery wit,ã explains the narôÙraôÙtor of the new video from Aeon above. ã
As women couldôÙnãt attend uniôÙverôÙsiôÙty, her uncle and guardian arranged for her to conôÙtinôÙue her eduôÙcaôÙtion with a renowned young scholôÙar.ã That, of course, wasô Abelard, who didôÙnãt need too much one-on-one time with his new pupil before decidôÙing to cast off his famousôÙly ascetic ways and roll the dice on love. Alas, we all know at least the more draôÙmatôÙic points of how it turned out: casôÙtraôÙtion for Abelard, self-imposed cloisôÙterôÙing for the both of them. Yet even that didôÙnãt mark the end of their assoôÙciôÙaôÙtion.
In her nunôÙhood, Hûˋloû₤se ãcame to posôÙsess a letôÙter Abelard intendôÙed to send to a friend, euloôÙgizôÙing their time togethôÙer. In response, she iniôÙtiôÙatôÙed a years-long corôÙreôÙsponôÙdence.ã The letôÙters ãare steeped in longôÙing, yet they tranôÙscend the sighs of star-crossed lovers, weavôÙing heart-wrenchôÙing perôÙsonôÙal senôÙtiôÙment with trailôÙblazôÙing theôÙolôÙoôÙgy and phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy.ã At one point, Hûˋloû₤se brings her philoôÙsophôÙiôÙcal mind to bear on the probôÙlem of their own relaôÙtionôÙship, arrivôÙing at her simulôÙtaôÙneôÙous guilt and innoôÙcence on the premise that ãit is not the deed, but the intenôÙtion of the doer, which makes the crime.ã Here we have an earôÙly examôÙple of what philosoôÙphers today call ãintenôÙtionôÙalôÙist,ã as opposed to ãconôÙseôÙquenôÙtialôÙist,ã ethics. How much comôÙfort her arguôÙment that ãthere can be no sin in an action done out of loveã proôÙvidôÙed Abelard is unclear. But sureôÙly he appreôÙciôÙatôÙed its intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal merôÙits, givôÙen that his mind, at least, was left wholôÙly intact.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
The EncyôÙcloôÙpeôÙdia of Women PhilosoôÙphers: A New Web Site Presents the ConôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions of Women PhilosoôÙphers, from Ancient to ModôÙern
The ConôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions of Women PhilosoôÙphers RecovôÙered by the New Project Vox WebôÙsite
A Short AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to HypaôÙtia, Ancient Alexandriaãs Great Female PhilosoôÙpher
An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the FemôÙiôÙnist PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy of Simone de BeauôÙvoir
Kierkegaard on Why We All MisôÙunôÙderôÙstand the True MeanôÙing of Love: An AniôÙmatôÙed ExplaôÙnaôÙtion
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Before Bill Nye, There Was Julius Sumner Miller: Watch Complete Episodes of His Classic Science Show, Why Is It So?, Free Online (1962ã73)
description:
in Physics, Science, Television |   	June 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

ãWe are approachôÙing a darkôÙness in the land. Boys and girls are eme
body:
in Physics, Science, Television |   	June 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

ãWe are approachôÙing a darkôÙness in the land. Boys and girls are emergôÙing from every levôÙel of school with cerôÙtifiôÙcates and degrees, but they canãt read, write or calôÙcuôÙlate. We donãt have acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic honôÙesty or intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal rigôÙor.ã That quote may sound like a familôÙiar lament today, but itãs actuôÙalôÙly drawn from an interôÙview conôÙductôÙed about half a cenôÙtuôÙry ago with the physiôÙcist and teleôÙviôÙsion host Julius SumôÙnôÙer Miller. If that name sounds familôÙiar to you, thereãs a fair chance youãre an AusôÙtralian who grew up between the sixôÙties and the eightôÙies ã and itãs hardôÙly imposôÙsiôÙble that, thanks to his proôÙgram Why Is It So?, you went on to purôÙsue a career in sciôÙence or engiôÙneerôÙing.
GenôÙerôÙaôÙtions of young viewôÙers down under and elseôÙwhere learned fromô Why Is It So? that physics and its prinôÙciôÙples could be fun. Even if you werenãt among them at the time, you can now watch full episodes of the show uploaded to YouTube by ABC, the AusôÙtralian BroadôÙcastôÙing CorôÙpoôÙraôÙtion.
As you may notice after just a few secôÙonds of lisôÙtenôÙing to him, Miller himôÙself was AmerôÙiôÙcan. The MassôÙaôÙchuôÙsetts-born son of immiôÙgrants from Latvia and LithuaôÙnia, he studôÙied physics at Boston UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty and thereôÙafter taught and perôÙformed research at varôÙiôÙous instiôÙtuôÙtions (befriendôÙing Albert EinôÙstein along the way) before takôÙing a long-term posiôÙtion at El Camino ColôÙlege in TorôÙrance, CalôÙiôÙforôÙnia in 1952.


	

?si=Xli51nEj2zgiS6UE
Millerãs popôÙuôÙlarôÙiôÙty at El Camino, the schoolãs proxôÙimôÙiôÙty to HolôÙlyôÙwood, and teleôÙviôÙsionãs rapid expanôÙsion into a mass mediôÙum led toô his launchôÙingô Why Is It So? on KNXT in Los AngeôÙles in 1959. By the mid-sixôÙties, he was also explainôÙing sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic pheôÙnomôÙeôÙna on DisôÙneyãsô MickôÙey Mouse Club, Great Moments in SciôÙence, and SciôÙence and Its MagôÙic, as well as on Steve Allenãs late-night talk show. He made his debut on AusôÙtralian teleôÙviôÙsion when the UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty of SydôÙney brought him out as a visôÙitôÙing lecôÙturôÙer. The appearôÙance went wrong when he couldôÙnãt perôÙform his stanôÙdard trick of driôÙving a drinkôÙing straw through a potaôÙto, but what it nevôÙerôÙtheôÙless got him ã apart from an office filled with the domesôÙtic straws heãd jokôÙingôÙly critôÙiôÙcized on-air ã was a new home for Why Is It So?ô on ABC.


	

ABC has so far made availôÙable sevôÙen full broadôÙcasts origôÙiôÙnalôÙly aired between the earôÙly sixôÙties and the earôÙly sevôÙenôÙties. Despiteô their black-and-white proôÙducôÙtion and lack of visuôÙal effects, they hold up well today in both eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal and enterôÙtainôÙment valôÙue.ô HowôÙevôÙer engagôÙing his perôÙsonôÙalôÙiôÙty as what we would now call a sciôÙence comôÙmuôÙniôÙcaôÙtor, it seems that ãMiller could be a terôÙror in the classôÙroom,ã accordôÙing to his Los AngeôÙlesô Times obitôÙuôÙary from 1987, ãintolôÙerôÙant of misôÙspelled words or misôÙplaced puncôÙtuôÙaôÙtionã and insisôÙtent that ãmost facôÙulôÙty were not rigid enough and that stuôÙdents were not learnôÙing enough.ã Heãd hardôÙly be pleased with whatãs hapôÙpened to intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal stanôÙdards in the nearôÙly four decades since his death, butô heãd sureôÙly appreôÙciôÙate thatô his teachôÙing conôÙtinôÙues to reach ãeveryôÙbody ages four to 94,ã as he liked to describe his audiôÙence. Age, nationôÙalôÙiôÙty, and even creôÙdenôÙtials didôÙnãt matôÙter; what countôÙed was genôÙuine curiosôÙiôÙty and the willôÙingôÙness to purôÙsue it, whether in the classôÙroom or the livôÙing room.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
The PioôÙneerôÙing Physics TV Show The MechanôÙiôÙcal UniôÙverse Is Now on YouTube: 52 ComôÙplete Episodes from CalôÙtech
Watch a Young Carl Sagan Appear in His First TV DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtary, The VioôÙlent UniôÙverse (1969)
Richard FeynôÙman EnthuôÙsiôÙasôÙtiôÙcalôÙly Explains How to Think Like a PhysiôÙcist in His Series Fun to ImagôÙine (1983)
The GreatôÙest Shot in TeleôÙviôÙsion: SciôÙence HisôÙtoôÙriôÙan James Burke Had One Chance to Nail This Scene ãÎ and Nailed It
The OffiôÙcial MisôÙter Rogersã NeighôÙborôÙhood YouTube ChanôÙnel Goes Live: Watch ComôÙplete Episodes, IncludôÙing the Very First
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Map of Physics: Animation Shows How All the Different Fields in Physics Fit Together
description:
in Physics |   	June 18th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

From Newtonãs mechanôÙiôÙcal calôÙcuôÙlaôÙtions to Einsteinãs genôÙerôÙal and speôÙcial relôÙaôÙtiv
body:
in Physics |   	June 18th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

From Newtonãs mechanôÙiôÙcal calôÙcuôÙlaôÙtions to Einsteinãs genôÙerôÙal and speôÙcial relôÙaôÙtivôÙiôÙty to the bafôÙfling indeôÙterôÙmiôÙnaôÙcy of quanôÙtum mechanôÙics, the disôÙciôÙpline of physics has become increasôÙingôÙly arcane and comôÙplex, and less and less govôÙerned by orderôÙly laws. This presents a probôÙlem for the layperôÙson, who strugôÙgles to underôÙstand how NewôÙtonôÙian physics, with its preôÙdictable obserôÙvaôÙtions of physôÙiôÙcal forces, relates to the parôÙalôÙlax and paraôÙdox of latôÙer disôÙcovôÙerôÙies. ãIf you donãt already know physics,ã says physiôÙcist Dominic WalôÙliôÙman in the video above, itãs difôÙfiôÙcult someôÙtimes to see how all of these difôÙferôÙent subôÙjects are relatôÙed to each othôÙer.ã So WalôÙliôÙman has proôÙvidôÙed a helpôÙful visuôÙal aid: an aniôÙmatôÙed video map showôÙing the conôÙnecôÙtions between clasôÙsiôÙcal physics, quanôÙtum physics, and relôÙaôÙtivôÙiôÙty.
Newtonãs laws of motion and gravôÙiôÙtaôÙtion and his invenôÙtion of calôÙcuôÙlus best repôÙreôÙsent the first domain. Here we see the insepôÙaôÙraôÙble relaôÙtionôÙship between physics and math, ãthe bedrock that the world of physics is built from.ã When we come to one of Newtonãs less well-known purôÙsuits, optics, we see how his interôÙest in light waves anticôÙiôÙpatôÙed James Clerk Maxwellãs work on elecôÙtroôÙmagôÙnetôÙic fields. After this iniôÙtial conôÙnecôÙtion, the proôÙlifôÙerôÙaôÙtion of subôÙdisôÙciôÙplines intenôÙsiôÙfies: fluôÙid mechanôÙics, chaos theôÙoôÙry, therôÙmoôÙdyôÙnamôÙicsãÎ the guidôÙing forceô of them all is the study of enerôÙgy in varôÙiôÙous states. The heurisôÙtics of clasôÙsiôÙcal physics preôÙvailed, and worked perôÙfectôÙly well, until about 1900, when the clockôÙwork uniôÙverse of NewôÙtonôÙian mechanôÙics explodôÙed with new probôÙlems, both at very large and very small levôÙels of descripôÙtion.
It is here that physics branchôÙes into relôÙaôÙtivôÙiôÙty and quanôÙtum mechanôÙics, which WalôÙliôÙman explains in brief. While we are likeôÙly familôÙiar with the very basics of Einsteinãs relôÙaôÙtivôÙiôÙty, quanôÙtum physics tends to get a litôÙtle less covôÙerôÙage in the typôÙiôÙcal course of a genôÙerôÙal eduôÙcaôÙtion, due to its comôÙplexôÙiôÙty, perôÙhaps, as well as the fact that at their edges, quanôÙtum explaôÙnaôÙtions fail. While quanôÙtum field theôÙoôÙry, says WalôÙliôÙman, is ãthe best descripôÙtion of the uniôÙverse we have,ã once we come to quanôÙtum gravôÙiôÙtaôÙtion, we reach ãthe giant Chasm of IgnoôÙranceã that specôÙuôÙlaôÙtive and conôÙtroôÙverôÙsial ideas like string theôÙoôÙry and loop quanôÙtum gravôÙiôÙty attempt to bridge.

At the ãChasm of IgnoôÙrance,ã our jourôÙney through the domains of physics ends, and we end up back in the airyô realmô where it all began, phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy. Those of us with a typôÙiôÙcal genôÙerôÙal eduôÙcaôÙtion in the sciôÙences may find that we have a much betôÙter underôÙstandôÙing of the fieldãs intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal geogôÙraôÙphy. As a handy reminder, you might even wish to purôÙchase a poster copy of Wallimanãs Map of Physics, which you can see en miniaôÙture above. (Itãs also availôÙable as a digôÙiôÙtal downôÙload here.) Just below, the charmôÙing, laid-back physiôÙcist takes the stage in a TEDx talk to demonôÙstrate effecôÙtive sciôÙence comôÙmuôÙniôÙcaôÙtion, explainôÙing ãquanôÙtum physics for 7 year olds,ã or, as it were, 37, 57, or 77-year olds.


	

Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
The StoôÙry of Physics AniôÙmatôÙed in 4 MinôÙutes: From Galileo and NewôÙton, to EinôÙstein
The FeynôÙman LecôÙtures on Physics, The Most PopôÙuôÙlar Physics Book Ever WritôÙten, Now ComôÙpleteôÙly Online
The Big Ideas of Stephen HawkôÙing Explained with SimôÙple AniôÙmaôÙtion
An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the Life & Work of Marie Curie, the First Female Nobel LauôÙreôÙate
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: Sour Soda Studio Depicts a Saccharine World Where Humans Have Lost Control
description: Think for a second about what comes to mind when you hear ãsoda.ã Perhaps fizzy, saccharine, and bright? Then consider the connotations of the wor
body:
Think for a second about what comes to mind when you hear ãsoda.ã Perhaps fizzy, saccharine, and bright? Then consider the connotations of the word ãsour.ã Maybe it evokes the zing of a lemon, tanginess, or something sharper. This is the relationship that forms the basis of Sour Soda Studio, a project built upon two decades of illustration experience with a playful and slightly unsettling view of some of the most pressing issues of the Anthropocene.
ãIt didnãt come from a change of direction, or from a manifesto,ã says the artist, who prefers to remain unnamed. ãIt came from something simpler: the need to say different things with a different voice.ã In these vibrant, often absurd works with titles like ãPlastic Windã and ãThe Sirenãs Catch,ã humansã control over their surroundings is just a fantasy. Clouds mimic the shapes of trees, tiny figures hold onto botanicals floating inside of bubbles, and totally oblivious festival-goers ignore a polar bearãs plight on a shrinking chunk of ice even as it mauls one of them to death.
ãGreen Northã
Sour Soda Studioãs approach is like a bit of a visual side-eye, nodding with an air of dark humor to the anxieties and societal disconnect around the climate crisis and humanityãs role in the balance of nature. A lumberjack whacks at a tree thatãs already on fire. A crocodile disappears into the brush with all but a pool cleanerãs arm. And mermaids are fished from the sea like tuna and later canned in attractive packaging. Arenãt sirens known for enticing humans into the depths?
The artist began by tinkering with ideas on paper, then rendering vectors on an iPad. Over time, what he describes as a ãvisual alphabetã began to emerge that consisted of simple forms and colors and a world of transitional landscapes and suspended figures, animals, and plants. Theyãre all ãimages that can be poetic, decorative, narrative, or something harder to name,ã he says. ãMany of them touch on nature, ecosystems, consumption, and the relationship between people and the world they live in.ã
See more on Behance.
ãThe Sirenãs Catch 1ã
ãThe Sirenãs Catch 2ã

ãPool Serviceã
ãFire Seasonã
ãClearingã
ãTrapped Cloudsã
ãPlastic Windã
ãDeliveryã
ãMigrationã

title: Surreal Figures Step from Leonora Carrington's Paintings into 'Shape of Dreams'
description: Raised in a wealthy, well-connected family in England, the young Leonora Carrington (1917-2011) glommed onto stories her mother and grandmother to
body:
Raised in a wealthy, well-connected family in England, the young Leonora Carrington (1917-2011) glommed onto stories her mother and grandmother told of Celtic folk tales about mythical beings in Ireland. Her imagination ran rampant as a child, and a rebellious spirit earned her expulsion from more than one convent school for antics like writing backwards and even trying to levitate. Later, her father insisted she be presented to the court of King George V at a debutante ball and was expected to ãmarry well.ã
Art and fantasy continued to call to Carrington, though, and not to be sallied by social convention, she attended the Chelsea School of Art, discovered Surrealism at the 1936 International Surrealist Exhibition, and developed a close relationship with artist Max Ernst. Over time, through Ernstãs connections, she got to know veritable art historical titans like Andrûˋ Breton, Salvador DalûÙ, Joan Mirû°, Pablo Picasso, and more.
ãGato de la Nocheã (2010), lost-wax bronze casting
The prolific artist may be known foremost for her otherworldly paintings, but the artistãs oeuvre extends far beyond the two-dimensional surface. Shape of Dreams at LãSPACE Gallery highlights Carringtonãs imaginative approach to sculpture and wearable art, particularly through large-scale lost-wax bronze sculptures and gold-plated jewelry. The vast majority of the sculptures were cast toward the end of Carringtonãs lifetime, with just a few made posthumously.
Dreamy surrealist fictions seeped into every aspect of Carringtonãs work. She was profoundly influenced by traumatic experiences during World War II, which led to a months-long stay in a psychiatric institution. Once she was discharged, she eventually settled in Mexico, where she lived in a kind of exile and made surreal work that investigates the nature of transience and uncertainty, especially through motifs like floating creatures and shifting landscapes. ãThere are exhibitions that begin with scholarship, and there are exhibitions that begin with intuition,ã the gallery says. ãShape of Dreams began with a simple but persistent question: what happens when the fantastical beings that inhabit Leonora Carringtonãs paintings step out of the canvas and into our world?ã
Indeed, the mythical, pagan character of the artistãs three-dimensional works are drawn directly from the figures in her paintings. Cloaked figures, strange masks, and human-animal hybrids populate a fantastical, magical world. ãThe sculptures appear almost in procession, as though Carringtonãs creatures, priestesses, hybrid animals, and dream-beings have stepped out of the pictorial plane and entered the gallery space,ã says a statement.
Shape of Dreams continues through July 25 in New York. You may also be interested in a new biopic titled Leonora in the Morning Light, plus the exhibition Leonora Carrington: Portrait of a Singular Artist, which continues through July 19 at the Musûˋe du Luxembourg.
Detail of ãGato de la Nocheã
ãThe Inventor of Atoleã (2011), lost-wax bronze casting, 51 x 23 x 31.5 inches, A/P edition
ãLooking Inã (2010), lost-wax bronze casting, 5 x 13 x 12.5 inches
ãBailarin,ã 24k gold-plate on 0.925 sterling silver, gemstones, 3 x 2 x 0.3 inches
ãLa Madre de los Lobosã (2008), lost-wax bronze casting
ãUnknownã (2010), lost-wax bronze casting, 42.5 x 19 x 20 inches, edition 7 of 10
ãCatwomanã (2011), lost-wax bronze casting, 36 x 11 x 24 inches, edition 8 of 10

title: 10 Art Events for Juneteenth Joy in NYC
description:
Guide
                David Hammons-inspired freedom flag workshops, a community art mosaic, late hours at the Bronx Museum, and
body:
Guide
                David Hammons-inspired freedom flag workshops, a community art mosaic, late hours at the Bronx Museum, and more activities to mark the contemplative holiday.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Rico Gatson's "Beacons: Audre Lorde" (2018) at the 167th Street subway station in the Bronx (photo courtesy Bronx Museum)The United States' Juneteenth federal holiday, commemorating the day emancipation reached Texas years after the proclamation of the end of slavery, turns five this Friday.Notably, this year, the public holiday's festivities coincide with the country's 250th anniversary, which has propelled a nationwide discourse about which American stories ought to be elevated. In New York, arts groups and organizations are honoring the contemplative and joyous date with events ranging from heritage walks and drag performances to DJ sets and museum exhibitions. Here's a selection of programming across the boroughs this long weekend.The Bronx Museum's Juneteenth CelebrationThe Bronx Museum, 1040 Grand Concourse, The Bronx Thursday, June 18, 6ã9pmThe Bronx Museum will keep its galleries open late on Tuesday night for a Juneteenth event co-organized by the political communications firm PR2Politics. This celebration will feature drumming performances, DJ sets, conversations, and after-hours access to the museum's current exhibition The Seventh AIM Biennial: Forms of Connection.The Flea Public Performance SeriesThe Flea, 20 Thomas Street, Tribeca and various locations, Manhattan June 18ã24A snapshot from The Flea's Juneteenth programming from last year (photo courtesy The Flea)The Flea Theater's annual Juneteenth free performance series returns, kicking off on Thursday night at South Street Seaport with artist Asia Stewart's ãafter air,ã a memorial to individuals trafficked into slavery via the port of New York in the early 1800s. Next Wednesday, June 24, the Flea will also hold a talk by sociologist Renûˋe T. White exploring the 250th anniversary of the United States. More details on performances and locations are available here. Studio Museum, 144 West 125th Street, Harlem, Manhattan Friday, June 19, 11amã8pmThe Studio Museum in Harlem (photo Noel Woodford, courtesy Studio Museum in Harlem)This family-friendly event marks the museum's first Juneteenth celebration since its long-awaited reopening earlier this year. Through a series of drop-in sessions, the institution is offering David Hammons-inspired freedom flag workshops, reflection sessions, sound baths, movement exercises, and a discussion on incarcerated elders. Some events require RSVP, here. 
                  
                      
                        Guide
                      
                      
                        Juneteenth
                      
                      
                        New York

schema.headline: 10 Art Events for Juneteenth Joy in NYC

title: Heritage Groups Sue Trump Over ãGarden of American Heroes"
description:
News
                The National Mall is ãnot a personal sandbox for each President to renovate however he likes,ã preservationists
body:
News
                The National Mall is ãnot a personal sandbox for each President to renovate however he likes,ã preservationists argue.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Christopher Columbus, whose statue Trump re-erected outside the White House this year, is among the figures who will be included in his "Garden of American Heroes." (photo Celal Gunes/Anadolu via Getty Images)
    
        
            Six heritage preservation groups in Washington, DC, are suing the Trump administration over its plans to create a sculpture garden devoted to American ãexceptionalismã on public land along the Potomac River.   In a lawsuit filed against the Department of the Interior on Monday, June 15, organizations including the National Parks Conservation Association and the Cultural Landscape Foundation asked a federal judge to halt Trump's plan to build a 250-statue ãNational Garden of American Heroes.ãThe president's plan to install the monument in the National Park Service-controlled West Potomac Park, a part of the National Mall and Memorial Parks, was first announced in May. The proposed construction is unlawful, the groups argue, because it violates several congressional restrictions on new developments and other preservation laws.ãCongress has made clear that the National Mall is a ãsubstantially completed work of civic art,ãã the lawsuit reads, ãnot a personal sandbox for each President to renovate however he likes.ãIn this week's legal filings, the organizations raised concerns that renderings for the project appear to reduce ãopen grassy areasã and remove recreational sports fields from the public park, which houses the iconic Jefferson Memorial. An aerial rendering of the National Garden of American Heroes posted by Trump in May (screenshot via Truth Social)Among the violations alleged in the lawsuit is the administration's apparent disregard for regulations governing the creation of new monuments in the nation's capital. Typically, a multi-step review and Congress's approval would be required for such a project, the filings state. Instead of moving through the approved channels, the groups claim the president is accelerating the project consistent with the ãadministrationãs track record of beginning overhauls of historic and civic spaces in the nationãs capital without warning.ãTrump announced his plans to build the bizarre sculpture garden during his first presidential term, but the proposal was overridden when Joe Biden took office. The garden would include a hodgepodge of characters from American history, including Martin Luther King, Hannah Arendt, and Frederick Douglass, as well as controversial figures such as Christopher Columbus and missionary JunûÙpero Serra, who led the colonial Spanish mission system in California. Historians have called the selection of honorees ãoddã and ãrandom.ãA spokesperson for the United States Department of the Interior claimed the organizations that sued the agency had track records of supporting Democratic political campaigns. ãIt isô beyondô comprehensionô why anyone would sue over an exhibition that celebrates American greatness by highlighting someô ofô the most pivotal figures in our nationãs history,ã the spokesperson told Hyperallergic in a statement. In his second term, Trump has reportedly diverted funds from the National Endowment for the Humanities (NEH) and the National Endowment for the Arts ã both of which slashed numerous arts grants in 2025 ã to finance his ãAmerican Heroesã project. The NEH has offered artists up to $600,000 for winning sculpture designs.
